search_queries_used: site:who.int scabies fact sheet symptoms transmission diagnosis close contacts
candidate_sources_rejected: automatic contact treatment, household instruction, medicine, dose, laundering, isolation, or outbreak action
automatic diagnosis, investigation, treatment, medication, dose, procedure, referral, escalation, or discharge instructions
search snippets, landing pages, and broad source-family matches without an exact applicable section
rejection_reasons: All public-health and treatment actions remain clinician- and pathway-directed.
Autonomous clinical output is outside this documentation-only research programme and was not mapped.
Only opened exact official documents and directly applicable sections were accepted as evidence.
population_applicability: Adults or children with close contact or possible exposure; institutions, outbreaks, infants, pregnancy, older people, immunosuppression, and crusted disease need qualified review.
setting_applicability: UAE community, primary, school, care-home, hospital, infection-control, and dermatology assessment.
UAE_applicability: WHO epidemiology and features are transferable, but UAE public-health, infection-control, medicine, school, facility, and contact-management pathways remain required.
recency_verification: The current WHO fact sheet dated 2023 was reviewed on 2026-07-14.
superseded_check: No newer WHO scabies fact sheet was identified.

evidence_items.direct_relationship: The exact WHO transmission and diagnosis section supports documenting a scabies-contact context, onset, relevant contact and household history, skin inspection and distribution without asserting infestation.
evidence_items.paraphrased_evidence_summary: Supports documenting close skin contact, household context, exposure before symptoms, characteristic distribution, and clinician-completed visual, dermoscopic, or microscopy assessment without inferring diagnosis.

evidence_items.direct_relationship: The exact symptom and population section supports recording itch, burrows, papules, pediatric distribution, immunosuppression, crusting and secondary infection only when assessed.
evidence_items.paraphrased_evidence_summary: Supports documenting itch, night worsening, burrows, papules, site distribution, infant or child distribution, immunosuppression, crusting, and secondary skin infection context.

unresolved_source_gaps: Exposure date and type, index case status, symptoms and distribution, household and institutional contacts, pregnancy and age, immune status, examination, diagnosis, microscopy or dermoscopy, medicine, contact management, infection control, and follow-up remain unsupported.
Any legacy item not explicitly mapped to an exact section remains unsupported pending qualified clinician review.
No mapping authorises an automatic diagnosis, differential, investigation, management decision, medicine, dose, procedure, referral, urgency conclusion, discharge decision, or patient instruction.
